quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Allosteric regulation"?

options.text: Regulatory molecules bind to a site other than the active site, changing the enzyme's conformation and activity; can be activating or inhibiting
v = Vmax[S]/(Km + [S]); describes the relationship between substrate concentration and reaction rate for many enzymes
No mutation, no migration, random mating, no selection, large population size; violations drive evolution
The energy available to do work; ΔG  0 (endergonic, requires energy input), ΔG = 0 (equilibrium)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Allosteric regulation" is defined as: Regulatory molecules bind to a site other than the active site, changing the enzyme's conformation and activity; can be activating or inhibiting. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Competitive inhibitor"?

options.text: A phospholipid bilayer with embedded proteins that controls what enters and exits the cell; selectively permeable
A cluster of prokaryotic genes under control of a single promoter and operator, transcribed as one polycistronic mRNA (e.g., lac operon, trp operon)
A molecule resembling the substrate that competes for the active site; increases apparent Km but does not affect Vmax (can be overcome by more substrate)
The molecular machine for translation; composed of large and small subunits containing rRNA and proteins; has A, P, and E sites

quiz_questions.explanation: "Competitive inhibitor" is defined as: A molecule resembling the substrate that competes for the active site; increases apparent Km but does not affect Vmax (can be overcome by more substrate). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Noncompetitive inhibitor"?

options.text: A molecule that binds to a site other than the active site, reducing enzyme activity by changing the active site's shape; decreases Vmax but Km is unchanged
Repair of a single damaged base: DNA glycosylase removes the damaged base, AP endonuclease cuts the backbone, and polymerase fills the gap
Germline mutations (in eggs/sperm) are passed to offspring; somatic mutations affect only the individual and are not inherited
A normal gene that promotes cell growth; mutations can convert it to an oncogene, causing uncontrolled cell division

quiz_questions.explanation: "Noncompetitive inhibitor" is defined as: A molecule that binds to a site other than the active site, reducing enzyme activity by changing the active site's shape; decreases Vmax but Km is unchanged. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Feedback inhibition"?

options.text: The end product of a metabolic pathway inhibits an early enzyme in the pathway, preventing overproduction (e.g., isoleucine inhibiting threonine deaminase)
2pq; the proportion of heterozygous carriers of a recessive allele in a population, estimable from disease frequency (q²)
A signaling molecule (hormone, neurotransmitter, growth factor) that binds specifically to a receptor protein
Ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate carboxylase/oxygenase; the most abundant enzyme on Earth; fixes CO₂ but also has oxygenase activity (photorespiration)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Feedback inhibition" is defined as: The end product of a metabolic pathway inhibits an early enzyme in the pathway, preventing overproduction (e.g., isoleucine inhibiting threonine deaminase). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Cofactors and coenzymes"?

options.text: RNA polymerase reads the DNA template strand 3'→5' and synthesizes complementary mRNA 5'→3'; occurs in the nucleus in eukaryotes
A DNA sequence upstream of a gene (e.g., TATA box) where RNA polymerase and transcription factors bind to initiate transcription
A gene-editing system using guide RNA to direct the Cas9 nuclease to cut DNA at a precise location, enabling precise gene knockout, correction, or insertion
Non-protein helpers required for enzyme function; cofactors are inorganic ions (Zn²⁺, Fe²⁺); coenzymes are organic molecules (NAD⁺, vitamins)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Cofactors and coenzymes" is defined as: Non-protein helpers required for enzyme function; cofactors are inorganic ions (Zn²⁺, Fe²⁺); coenzymes are organic molecules (NAD⁺, vitamins). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Regulatory molecules bind to a site other than the active site, changing the enzyme's conformation and activity; can be activating or inhibiting"?

options.text: Reproductive isolation
Allosteric regulation
Photorespiration
Ecosystem services

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Allosteric regulation".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A molecule resembling the substrate that competes for the active site; increases apparent Km but does not affect Vmax (can be overcome by more substrate)"?

options.text: Michaelis-Menten kinetics
Competitive inhibitor
Pre-mRNA processing
Meiosis

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Competitive inhibitor".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A molecule that binds to a site other than the active site, reducing enzyme activity by changing the active site's shape; decreases Vmax but Km is unchanged"?

options.text: Noncompetitive inhibitor
Species richness
Krebs cycle (citric acid cycle)
Ecosystem services

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Noncompetitive inhibitor".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The end product of a metabolic pathway inhibits an early enzyme in the pathway, preventing overproduction (e.g., isoleucine inhibiting threonine deaminase)"?

options.text: Dominant and recessive
Nucleus
Feedback inhibition
Missense mutation

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Feedback inhibition".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Non-protein helpers required for enzyme function; cofactors are inorganic ions (Zn²⁺, Fe²⁺); coenzymes are organic molecules (NAD⁺, vitamins)"?

options.text: r-selected species
Allele frequency
Cofactors and coenzymes
Carrying capacity (K)

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Cofactors and coenzymes".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Regulation of Enzyme Activity is accurate?

options.text: Regulation of Enzyme Activity controls metabolic pathways through allosteric regulation, competitive and noncompetitive inhibition, and feedback inhibition.
Conservation Biology integrates ecology, genetics, and evolutionary biology to protect and restore biological diversity.
Population viability analysis (PVA) estimates extinction probability for small populations considering demographic and environmental stochasticity.
The central dogma of molecular biology—DNA → RNA → Protein—describes the flow of genetic information in cells.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Regulation of Enzyme Activity as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Regulation of Enzyme Activity?

options.text: The Knudson two-hit hypothesis explains that both copies of a tumor suppressor must be inactivated for cancer to develop.
Minimum viable population (MVP), effective population size (Ne), and genetic rescue inform conservation management.
Monosaccharides join via glycosidic bonds through dehydration synthesis to form disaccharides and polysaccharides.
Allosteric regulators bind to sites other than the active site, changing the enzyme's conformation to activate or inhibit it.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Regulation of Enzyme Activity as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Regulation of Enzyme Activity?

options.text: Population Genetics provides the mathematical framework for studying evolution using allele and genotype frequencies.
Water is split (photolysis) at PSII, releasing O₂.
Competitive inhibitors resemble the substrate and compete for the active site, while noncompetitive inhibitors bind elsewhere and reduce catalytic activity.

Translation occurs in three stages: initiation (ribosome assembles at start codon AUG), elongation (amino acids added one by one as ribosome moves along mRNA), and termination (release factor binds stop codon).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Regulation of Enzyme Activity as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Regulation of Enzyme Activity?

options.text: Water is split (photolysis) at PSII, releasing O₂.
Evolution and Evidence establishes that populations change over time through multiple converging lines of evidence.
Environmental factors (temperature, pH, salt concentration) also affect enzyme activity.
Monohybrid crosses (Aa × Aa → 3:1 phenotypic ratio) and dihybrid crosses (AaBb × AaBb → 9:3:3:1 ratio) predict offspring ratios, testable with chi-square analysis.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Regulation of Enzyme Activity as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Regulation of Enzyme Activity?

options.text: Regulation of Enzyme Activity controls metabolic pathways through allosteric regulation, competitive and noncompetitive inhibition, and feedback inhibition.
Monohybrid crosses (Aa × Aa → 3:1 phenotypic ratio) and dihybrid crosses (AaBb × AaBb → 9:3:3:1 ratio) predict offspring ratios, testable with chi-square analysis.
Nucleic Acids (DNA and RNA) store and transmit genetic information through sequences of nucleotide monomers.
Adaptive radiation (rapid diversification from a single ancestor into multiple ecological niches) often follows mass extinctions or colonization of new environments.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Regulation of Enzyme Activity as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Regulation of Enzyme Activity?

options.text: Insulin signaling pathway: insulin binding to its receptor triggers a kinase cascade leading to glucose transporter (GLUT4) insertion into cell membranes
Captive breeding and reintroduction programs recovering the California condor from 22 individuals (1982) to over 500, including 300+ in the wild
Aspirin acting as an irreversible inhibitor of cyclooxygenase (COX), blocking prostaglandin synthesis and reducing pain, inflammation, and blood clotting
The Human Genome Project using genetic and physical mapping to determine the positions of all ~20,000 human genes across 23 chromosome pairs

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Regulation of Enzyme Activity.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Regulation of Enzyme Activity?

options.text: Brewing and winemaking using yeast alcohol fermentation to convert glucose to ethanol and CO₂

Wolf reintroduction into Yellowstone (1995) demonstrating trophic cascades: wolves reduced elk overgrazing, allowing riparian vegetation recovery, which stabilized stream banks and increased beaver populations
Yellowstone-to-Yukon Conservation Initiative creating wildlife corridors spanning 3,200 km to connect fragmented habitats for grizzly bears, wolves, and elk migration
Lead poisoning: lead (Pb²⁺) replaces zinc in enzymes, acting as a noncompetitive inhibitor and disrupting nervous system, kidney, and blood cell function

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Regulation of Enzyme Activity.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Regulation of Enzyme Activity applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Feedback inhibition in the cholesterol synthesis pathway: statins mimic the substrate of HMG-CoA reductase, acting as competitive inhibitors to lower cholesterol levels
Cancer epigenetics: abnormal DNA methylation patterns silencing tumor suppressor genes or activating oncogenes without DNA mutations
Forensic genetics using Mendelian inheritance patterns to establish paternity or identify family relationships from DNA evidence
Artificial photosynthesis research mimicking PSII's water-splitting capability to generate hydrogen fuel using sunlight

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Regulation of Enzyme Activity.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Regulation of Enzyme Activity?

options.text: Telomere shortening with each cell division limiting cell lifespan; telomerase activation in cancer cells enables unlimited replication (immortality)
Exercise physiology: transitioning from aerobic to anaerobic respiration during intense activity when O₂ delivery cannot meet demand
Prion diseases (mad cow, CJD) where misfolded proteins induce normal proteins to refold incorrectly, demonstrating the critical importance of protein conformation
Aspirin acting as an irreversible inhibitor of cyclooxygenase (COX), blocking prostaglandin synthesis and reducing pain, inflammation, and blood clotting

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Regulation of Enzyme Activity.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Regulation of Enzyme Activity in practice?

options.text: Madagascar's unique biodiversity (90% of species found nowhere else) explained by 88 million years of isolation after separating from Africa and India
Artificial photosynthesis research mimicking PSII's water-splitting capability to generate hydrogen fuel using sunlight
Lead poisoning: lead (Pb²⁺) replaces zinc in enzymes, acting as a noncompetitive inhibitor and disrupting nervous system, kidney, and blood cell function
Amazon deforestation averaging ~10,000 km² per year, threatening the most biodiverse terrestrial ecosystem with an estimated 10% of all species on Earth

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Regulation of Enzyme Activity.
